title: Things We Know
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Things We Know - Pastor Tito Lyro - 1 John 5_18-21.txt
outline: Introduction: The conclusion of 1 John, 'the end of our series'
Three things we know: 1) The born-again do not practice sin
2) We are of God, the whole world lies in the wicked one
3) The Son of God has come and given us understanding
Knowing Him who is true — in His Son Jesus Christ
The command: keep yourselves from idols
Conclusion: The certainty and assurance of faith in Christ
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro preaches the conclusion of the 1 John series from 5:18-21, presenting three certainties that John affirms for believers. Those born of God do not practice sin; we are of God while the world lies in the evil one; and the Son of God has come to give us understanding of the true God.
transcript:
## Introduction

Coming to the end of a book in your preaching is happy and sad at the same time. You get attached to the book you are preaching from. This is the 23rd message from the book of 1 John, and we are finishing it in God's good providence today. It is a little bit like a kid leaving your house on good terms — you are happy that it happened and that they are following the Lord, and so on, but you are going to miss it. It is unlike a faithful pastor who preaches through the Bible and remains in the same church for a long time — he will rarely preach a book twice in his life. So once you finish it, you have kind of done it for the rest of your life if you stay in the same church. So we say a fond farewell to 1 John today.

As John brings this letter to the beloved children in Ephesus — remember he is writing this letter likely to the Church of Ephesus, where he was the pastor — he is bringing it to a church that had a hall-of-fame history. It was founded by Paul, pastored by Apollos, followed by Timothy, and then John. The next guy after that hall-of-fame team is doomed to struggle. And he is bringing this letter to a church that he obviously loves. You can see that every time he refers to them as "my little children." He considered himself their father and loved them with a fatherly love.

As he comes to the end of this first epistle, he reviews with them all the articles of faith he covered throughout the letter — all the things he taught them. And there are three things he wants to make sure they remember as they finish considering this letter. All three things have to do with what the Christian knows. If you notice as you read, there are three times that John says "we know." So he ends this letter with these three things he wants the Christian to know.

Remember verse 13 of chapter 5, where he told us the whole purpose of this letter: that we may know that we know Jesus Christ — to give us assurance of faith, assurance of salvation, and perseverance in faith. And here are three things the Christian knows. Three things we can take to the bank. John ends this letter with three clear and candid certainties. That is what we are going to see today.

## The First Certainty: Regeneration Results in Sanctification

> *"We know that whoever is born of God does not sin, but he who has been born of God keeps him, and the wicked one does not touch him."* (1 John 5:18)

We know that regeneration results in sanctification. The one who has been born of God is the one who has come to spiritual life. Throughout the Bible we have this idea of a new birth, and the Bible uses different terms to describe it. In Ezekiel 36, the same idea is taught in the concept of a heart transplant — God says He is going to reach into the hearts of His people and replace the heart of stone with a heart of flesh. That is the same concept as in verse 18. In John 3, Jesus says that unless you are born from above, you cannot know Him. Paul says in 2 Corinthians 5 that this new birth is a new creation — if you have been born again in Christ, all things are new, the old things have passed away. In Ephesians 2, Paul talks about coming to life: "You who were dead in your trespasses and sins, He has made alive in Jesus Christ." And in Colossians, Paul speaks of this coming to spiritual life in terms of being delivered from the power of the kingdom of darkness and being placed as a citizen of the kingdom of the Son whom God loves.

All these terms are ways to express the doctrine of regeneration — of being born of God. And if you follow Jesus Christ, that is who you are. You are born of God.

And a person who has been born again will not keep on sinning. The way the expression "does not sin" is written is a continuing action. Those born of God do not keep on sinning — their life is not marked by continual sinning. Now, "not keeping on sinning" does not refer to the absence of sin in general, but to particular sins. The one born again will not keep on committing the same sins over and over without any repentance. Their life will not be characterized by a particular sin. That is what John tells us.

This does not mean that the one born again will not struggle with sin — but it will be a struggle. Do you get the difference? John is not saying the believer will never sin, but that the believer will struggle with sin. We will not give in to sin without a fight. When he or she falls into sin, they will repent and walk with the Lord. It is going to be a struggle — not a comfortable living-with-sin. Spurgeon says, sin and the child of God are incompatible. They may occasionally meet, but they cannot live together in harmony.

So if you are living in harmony with sin, if you are okay with sin in your life, if the gospel is not driving you to repentance of particular sins, look at your heart and see if Jesus is actually there. The idea that a person can be born of God and continue unchanged regarding sin is not scriptural. It is contrary to what the Bible teaches. And it was exactly what the false teachers were teaching the church — the teaching that you can be born of God and yet live as if nothing changed.

There was a debate in the church in America about the possibility of accepting Jesus as your Savior but not accepting Jesus as your Lord. One side was generally represented by Zane Hodges, a professor at Dallas Theological Seminary. On the other side, arguing that it is a package deal — Lord and Savior or nothing — was John MacArthur, the pastor at Grace Community Church in California. This is exactly what this verse is teaching. You cannot come to Jesus as your Savior if He is not also your Lord.

Remember the account of the Philippian jailer in Acts 16. Silas and Paul were arrested, in jail, singing hymns at midnight in fetters. An earthquake came, the doors opened, and the jailer thought all the prisoners had run away. He was about to kill himself. Paul called out, "No, everyone is here! Don't do that!" And the jailer came to the point: "What must I do to be saved?" Remember the answer Paul gave him? Not "Believe in Jesus Christ" — but "Believe in the Lord Jesus Christ, and you will be saved, you and your household." Faith is in this person whose title is the Lord. That is what it means to believe in Jesus Christ — that He is the master of your life, that your life is being changed, and you are now serving Him.

No child of God is identified by his or her sin. That is not who you are. That is not what your life displays. You are a child of God, born of God, a Christian. That is your identity — not your sin. Old things have passed away. All things are new. You are a new creature. And the badge on that new creature is Christian — one who belongs to Christ, not one who belongs to sin. You have been crucified with Christ, and the life you now live is Christ living in you. So do not define yourself by sin, because you have been born from above.

### Jesus Protects Those Born of God

The reason John gives us that the one born of God does not keep on sinning is that Jesus protects him. Look at verse 18 again. John is playing on words. In our translation it is not entirely clear, but "the one born of God" at the beginning of the verse is the Christian, and "the one born of God" in the second half of the verse is Christ. Instead of "keeps himself," the words should be "keeps him" — Christ, the eternal Son of God, keeps the one who has been born of God. It is Jesus who stands for you. Jesus is the one who keeps you. The Lord Jesus Christ — fully God and fully man — protects those born again. And that is you.

You are so united with Christ that what you do, Christ is involved in, and Christ is not going to allow you to remain in sin. He protects you. And though Satan tries hard, notice how verse 18 ends: "the wicked one does not touch him." If you are born of God, the wicked one will not touch you. But he is going to try. Peter describes him as a roaring lion, seeking whom he may devour. You and I are going to experience the full force of Satan's attack — he does not spend much time on those who don't believe; they already belong to him. But Peter says in 1 Peter 5:8-10, "Be sober, be vigilant, because your adversary the devil walks about like a roaring lion, seeking whom he may devour. Resist him, steadfast in the faith" — and then, "may the God of all grace, who called you to His eternal glory by Christ Jesus, after you have suffered a while, perfect, establish, strengthen, and settle you."

And Jude breaks forth in the doxology at the very end of his epistle — he talks about Christ in these terms in Jude 24: "Now to Him who is able to keep you from stumbling, and to present you faultless before the presence of His glory with great joy." Can you believe that, brother and sister? Jesus Christ, who knows you thoroughly, is pleased to present you before the Father with great joy.

So that is the first certainty: regeneration leads to sanctification. You cannot be born of God if you are not being conformed to the image of His Son. If your life is not moving toward His Son, there is no reason to believe you are born of God.

## The Second Certainty: The Line Between the Kingdom of God and the Kingdom of Satan Is Clear

> *"We know that we are of God, and the whole world lies under the sway of the wicked one."* (1 John 5:19)

We know that the lines of separation are clearly drawn between the kingdom of God and the kingdom of Satan. In Christ Jesus, we are from God — literally, out of God. That is the image of birth. We came to spiritual life from God. And as we have seen throughout this letter, faith in the whole Christ, obedience to the commands of God, and love for the brethren result in assurance of salvation. Assurance is possible for us.

And because we are out of God, and the world is under the power of Satan, there is great enmity between us and the world. Brothers and sisters, we do not live in a friendly world. Right here John says the line is clear. This is the people of God; this is the world; and there is no friendship between the two.

This is very important. Systems of belief and worldviews that are not controlled by Christ — that are not in submission to Christ — are under the control of Satan. These are the only two teams playing the game: Christ and Satan. There is no one on the bench. You are either on Christ's team or on Satan's team.

John says the world is in the grip of the evil one, the ruler of this world — a title Jesus Himself uses in John 14. But when you feel the tugs of the world on your heart, remember: this world and its ruler are passing away. At best, this world is kindling for fire. So when you think, "I am going to denounce Christ in order to follow the world and Satan," all you are doing is giving up eternal life for kindling, because all of it is going to be burned up and recreated at the coming of the Lord Jesus Christ.

That is why in chapter 2, verses 15 through 17, John urges us not to love the world — the lust of the eye, the lust of the flesh, the pride of life — because all these things are passing away. But the one who does the will of God abides forever. You belong to Him.

Any system of belief and behavior that does not acknowledge Christ as the Lord of the universe is not neutral — it is raging against Christ and raging against you as a Christian. That is what Psalm 2 tells us: the nations are plotting against God and His Messiah, raging against them, trying to throw off the fetters that bind them to God. People think that by rebelling against God they can be free — they can be the captain of their own ship, the master of their own soul. But all humanity is in servitude to a master: either master sin or master righteousness. You are a slave. No matter how rebellious you are, you are a slave — to a very cruel master, sin and Satan.

Paul says in Romans 6:17-18:

> *"God be thanked that though you were slaves of sin, yet you obeyed from the heart that form of doctrine to which you were delivered. And having been set free from sin, you became slaves of righteousness."*

Christian, you are a servant. Who are you going to serve — Christ or sin?

We are not to embrace these worldly systems of doctrine, nor to be indifferent about them. We are to tear them down. Paul says we are not in a physical battle — we are in a spiritual warfare, and we are to use our minds in the power of God to pull down these strongholds, casting down arguments and every high thing that exalts itself against the knowledge of God, bringing every thought into captivity to the obedience of Christ.

Is your mind controlled by Christ? Do you spend time thinking through how every area of life must be brought into submission? How do you think about marriage? About work? About your free time? Every area of life has to be governed with the mind of Christ. Are you doing that? Or are you mindlessly submitting yourself to the systems of this age?

The line is clear: Christ and Satan. There is no "it's complicated." It is black and white. Where are you?

## The Third Certainty: True Knowledge Comes Through Jesus Christ

> *"And we know that the Son of God has come and has given us an understanding, that we may know Him who is true; and we are in Him who is true, in His Son Jesus Christ. This is the true God and eternal life."* (1 John 5:20)

We know that true and ultimate knowledge comes through Jesus Christ. Through Him we understand the Scriptures, we understand creation, and we understand providence. Apart from Christ you cannot figure out life. Full knowledge about anything is only attainable through Jesus Christ.

In Proverbs 1:7, we are told that the fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom, and that fear cannot happen unless you have faith in Jesus Christ. The fool is the one who refuses to believe there is a God. As Psalm 14:1 and 53:1 tell us, the fool says in his heart, "There is no God" — it is a continual action, the fool has to keep on telling himself, "There is no God," because if he stopped, the truth that God exists is so powerful it would just overcome him. The fool keeps saying it because he does not want to admit that his only hope is to become a servant of Jesus Christ.

God cannot be known apart from His Son. That is the only way to know God. We do not worship the same God as the Jews, or the Muslims, or the Mormons, or Jehovah's Witnesses, or liberal Christianity — because the only way to know God is through His Son. If you say you follow a God but are not in submission to Jesus Christ, that is a false god, and it is not going to deliver on its promises.

The Apostle Paul says that Christ is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn over all creation. It is through Him that we know God.

The ultimate purpose of understanding is to know the Father and the Son. Everything in your life is happening so that you can know Christ, know God better. Paul, as the representative Christian, says the goal of life is to strive to know Christ better. He lists all his pedigree in Philippians 3 — if anyone wants to rest on his laurels, Paul would beat all of us. Born in the tribe of Benjamin, the tribe that gave Israel its first king. Circumcised on the eighth day according to the law. A Hebrew of the Hebrews — his family spoke Hebrew when no one else did. Concerning the law, a Pharisee. In zeal, he persecuted the church. But he counts all that as garbage. He counts the fact that he met Christ on the road. He says:

> *"I have suffered the loss of all things, and count them as rubbish, that I may gain Christ and be found in Him, not having my own righteousness, which is from the law, but that which is through faith in Christ, the righteousness which is from God by faith."* (Philippians 3:8-9)

Are you pursuing Christ? Is He the desire of your life? Is He so precious that you are discarding everything else in life to pursue Him? The God whose Son is Jesus Christ is the only true God. Every other God is a false God. Are you pursuing the true God through Jesus Christ?

## Conclusion: Keep Yourselves from Idols

And then John brings this letter to an abrupt end — no "grace be with you," no parting pleasantry. He finishes by saying, "Little children, keep yourselves from idols. Amen."

This abrupt ending is not what one would expect. Yet John wants to leave his beloved little children with this command: keep yourselves from idolatry. Flee from these false gods that tempt you to embrace them. The idols John wants them to stay away from are any notion of God that is not the God of the Bible. That is what he has been teaching throughout the epistle. Run from anything that is not Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.

As we finish this epistle, listen to John: keep yourselves from false notions concerning God. And the best way to do that is to know God better. The best way to keep yourself from idols is to know God better. That is what we try to do in this series, and what we try to do every time we teach or preach. There is no better, no greater, no more important subject of study than the study of God Himself.

The study of God must always lead to conformity to the Son. We have a tendency to construct God after our own image. When we are confronted with something from the Scriptures, we say, "Well, that is not God at all" — but actually, that is you. We have a tendency to construct God in our own image. But to keep ourselves from idols, we study God so that we are conformed to the express image of the Godhead, the Lord Jesus Christ. To know Jesus is to know God. To know Jesus is to have assurance of your salvation and eternal destiny.

So as John urged his church, as we put everything we have learned from him in this first epistle — beloved brethren, flee from idolatry. Keep yourselves from idols. Know God and pursue Christ." You get a girl attached to the book that you are preaching from. This is the 23rd message from the book of 1st John and we are finishing it in God's good providence today.
